front: What is the fundamental principle of a Stack data structure?
back: Last-In, First-Out (LIFO). The last element added to the stack is the first one to be removed.

front: What are the two primary operations used to modify a stack and what do they do?
back:
Push: Adds a new element to the top of the stack.
Pop: Removes and returns the top element from the stack.

front: What is the 'stack overflow' condition when implementing a stack with an array?
back: It occurs when an attempt is made to push an element onto a stack that has already reached its maximum capacity (i.e., the top pointer equals the last index of the array).

front: What is the 'stack underflow' condition when implementing a stack with an array?
back: It occurs when an attempt is made to pop an element from an empty stack (i.e., the top pointer is at its initial, 'empty' position, typically -1).

front: What is the time complexity of the Push and Pop operations in an array-based stack implementation?
back: O(1) - Constant Time. Both operations only involve accessing or modifying a single element (the top) and updating the top pointer, regardless of the stack's current size.
